item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The first image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed. The second image was made for the article, and thus is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On re-reading, something bugs me about that afterword.
The field team seems to really be phoning it in on this follow-up.

The former landlord "refused to discuss them in any detail"!?
We're talking about a man who may have personal knowledge of four uncontained additional instances of a skip; not to mention potentially valuable insight on how such a blatant anomaly went undetected for decades.
And that's all they had to report. "The superintendent was uncooperative. So this is a dead end."
Where's the forensic analysis of the flat? Where's the interviews with neighbors? For that matter, where's the building manager's additional testimony after a few minutes in a back room with a rubber hose?

At the very least, this needs one more line something like: Mr. <BLACKBOX> has been remanded for additional questioning and further investigations are ongoing.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Agreed with Magnus. Additionally, there's some very non-humorous implications about what apparently happens in your standard MC&D-hosted event in the interview log.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
I would disagree.

This doesn't really fit the tone or content of a joke skip. It's a mainlister. There's no joke here, it's something created or acquired by MC&D and sold to a patron.
This would be better off as a Joke skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sadly, Wellington is no longer the best-dressed horse in London.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "talking animal" trope is one of my favorites on the site, but that being said, the interview didn't really sell it for me. It seems like the interview here seems to establish three things:

  • The character of the skip.
  • The background info on the skip and the larger implications thereof.
  • The humor of the skip, without needing to be a -J.

So on those grounds, I'm not much of a fan of the character, though the background info is neat. The humor… it's not really that funny or clever to me, and I just shrugged and said whatever (though including an image of the note is a nice touch). No-vote, because everything else is fine by me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I think I got pretty close, considering this was one year before BoJack Horseman came around :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, he's just awesome. +1 well deserved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. This is amazing.

The Cockney accent's nice too. Plus the awesome name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Wellington G. Wonderhorse"

Aaaaand upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey.

Comparing yourself to a nazi, for any reason?

Not cool.

Don't do that shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, for…! I saw this issue brought up earlier and didn't feel the need to necro things, but honestly! Several people reply with sarcasm, no one replies with a thorough explanation. SCP NAZI TO THE RESCUE! (BTW, if anyone can ever find a reference to any sort of Nazi coming "to the rescue" of anything anywhere else, I'll actually post that J-SCP I've been working on.)

So here's how this works: The SCP Foundation contains anything that would throw the general, public idea of what is "normal" and/or "possible" into absolute chaos. It doesn't have to be scary or dangerous, it just has to be inexplicable by modern science. This horse certainly qualifies there. If people knew about it, the implications would cause a very dramatic disturbance among the general public of the world. While we're on the subject, MC&D makes use of things that the Foundation would want to contain because they're impossible and people pay money just to experience commodities (think of people who pay to see magic shows), so MC&D's use of him further indicates that Mr. Wonderhorse is the sort of thing the Foundation would want to contain.

Granted, there are -Js that work that way as well (the ghost sign comes to mind…though honestly, I think that particular one could work as a mainlist article); what separates -Js from mainlist articles is not humor in and of itself, but rather the nature of the humor used. Is the joke a jab at some aspect of the Foundation itself, such as the fact that some things were once inexplicable by science but this has since changed (i.e. fire), or just a downright juvenile parody of SCP articles in general (like the butt ghost)? Is it a satire of a common commodity that some might consider illogical, such as the superiority of titanium or a fear of bugs? Is it a humorous reference to a relatively new or somewhat odd cultural convention such as emoticons, the Cool-Aid Man, or the concept of "grimdark"? The J-SCP I myself am working on is semi-satirical humor based on the idea that the Foundation must contain all things that would cause chaos if made publicly known. And I still say SCP-1059 ought to be a -J, since it's a satire on SCP noobs's first articles containing excess expungements because they think it makes articles better just by being used. Also, for the most part, J-SCPs are things the public does know about, but which are arguably weird nonetheless (not all, but many, which adds to the joke). But does Mr. Wonderhorse qualify for any one of those categories, or something analogous to any of them? I think you'll find, if you think about it, that he does not. That is why this is not a -J.

You are welcome, person who was flamed with sarcasm but never had this gently but thoroughly explained. I hope you are still around to see this, and that others with the same question for this or other articles will see this and be spared your previous fate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the Doctor is now being played by a horse

Hmmm. "Capaldi" does sound like a breed of horse. And as for "Clara Oswin Oswald"….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A horse with a top hat?
Upvoted
This is a bleedin' isthmus, it is. Much money in the's to everyone 'oo castled this porto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good luck with the Rhyming Slang.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably the closest the SCP Foundation will get to a canon "My Little Pony" entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every time I upvote a talking animal, I swear it's the last time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…I now love this even more.

"Sir, we've interviewed the entire neighborhood and every one of them put their hoof on a bible and swore they knew nothing about any horses. I'm afraid this is a dead end."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm, I see. Yeah, I can see it. Like, let's say, a talking horse taking a plane or ship to America and starring some rom com series.

Now, if you'll excuse me, I'll be avoiding the tomatoes. The SCP ones, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My intent, basically, was to imply that there actually is a massive population of sapient talking horses inhabiting the British Isles, which for some reason or another (let's call it an SEP Field) nobody has noticed. It's probable that people see them on the world news or on TV shows every day, and yet, for some reason, they never quite notice that the Doctor is now being played by a horse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would definitely think that they're at the very least looking for his family, talking horses pretty much require containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ye know, I took a glance at this, then read some of the discussion, and I was so sold on the idea that this was an incredibly funny mainlister, which is perfect okay in my book.

And then he described the show.

Dear lord. I need to read more MC&D material.

(And by the way, wouldn't the Foundation be more than willing to "fly over" his wife and kids? Or is it the case that they just couldn't locate them even with his help?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nyeah

NEIGH-yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
refused to discuss them in any detail, stating that they had been "nothing but trouble" during their entire stay

"And don't even get me started on the hay. Bloody hell, it was everywhere. EVERYWHERE! Who in this god-forsaken world needs so much hay?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am amused by the implication made at the end of the interview that there might have well *been* a family of horses renting a flat in Whitechapel. That + overall quality of the article = a well-deserved upvote. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll be real: "Goofy talking animal" does nothing for me when it's the only draw of an article. This a cute character and well-done, but I don't get why the concept keeps cropping up as an SCP. It doesn't work as one.

Downvoting with apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would depend where the doctor was from exactly. He would be much more likely to call him a regional nickname, or at the very least "northerner" or "southerner".

Perhaps he could say "at least you're not another bleedin' septic." or "nice to see someone from the old green and pleasant".

For what it's worth, cockney rhyming slang is, at it's root, a kind of thief-cant and is intended to obfuscate your meaning from outsiders. You wouldn't generally use the whole phrase, and particularly not the part that rhymes with your intended meaning, eg: "get up them apples" where the full phrase is apples and pears (stairs).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, so what term would be accurate for a talking Cockney horse to confirm that the doctor is of the same nationality as he?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, now that it's been brought up, you are kind of correct. Cockneys may have it differently, but there isn't any such thing as British nationality. We just identify each-other by the respective countries we come from within Britain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for the same line. Also Brits don't call each other Brits :P

item.title: Re: HOLY SHIT GUYS
item.content:encoded: GOO GOO GOO JOO©

item.title: Re: HOLY SHIT GUYS
item.content:encoded: Walrus?

item.title: HOLY SHIT GUYS
item.content:encoded:
IF IT'S NOT SRS AND SCRY, THEN IT MUST BE A J

THIS MISTAKE MUST BE RECTIFIED IMMEDIATELY

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You should be in the -j section.

Nyeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Waaaaaah this SCP isn't scary and SCPs are supposed to be scary and nothing funny ever ever ever ever happens in real life! It must be a 'J! We can't have humor intruding upon the grimderpness that is the mainlist!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it funny?

Is it meant to be funny?

Was it written in an interesting way that is proper for the mainlist?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People, it's a GOD DAMN TALKING HORSE.
Isn't anyone going to question why it's not in the -j section?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what this is, but I like it. At first I thought that MC&D had aquired him from an alternate timeline, but the "afterword" section made me go "wait what". Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
o u :V

Don't make me write fanfic. I'll fucking do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: also known as: a joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe Smapti was the one up for deletion, so, um…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They aren't really going to delete it, are they?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconding deletion. The Physical Plant has already laid in a request for any glue made from the hide.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconded :|

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Recommending deletion of Smapti at negative fucks given.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ On the other hand, though...
- This SCP is now 20% cooler.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was on the fence with this one, because its a talking horse and that doesn't scream "original", but the clinical handling of it was so exquisite I upvoted in the end. +1

Also it says it was forced to perform acts by our friends at Marshall. Squick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Blank flank!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But he doesn't even have a cutie mark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It just hit me. This is a SCP of a My Little Pony OC.

People, I believe we have just been had.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
- Sees horse wearing top hat.
- Upvotes.
Yes, I am that shallow sometimes. But come ON.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to hate it, but was unable to do so. >.<

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's to a true gentleman and scholar. But enough about me.

Fine job on this. The shark-punching center is richer for it. Somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Semi-reluctant upvote. I'm starting to get sick of talking animals, but I enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As far as I've been able to make out, Cockney slang is used in whichever way most confuses and/or irritates the listener.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotcha, learn something new everyday.

In this case, I think going with that more authentic cockney would make it nearly impossible for those of us with only a passing familiarity to follow the dialouge. Obfuscation is the point of the slang, after all.

Second possiblity, maybe Mr. Ed…er..Wellington isn't authentically cockney. He could have just been raised/indoctrinated that way because his owners found it amusing. There is no external collaboration for his backstory after all. But being posh types, the trainers wouldn't neccesarily know how to do it right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know how cockney rhyming slang works, it's just you don't often use both words in the rhyme. If I'm on the phone I'll say to someone 'Hang on, I'm on the dog', I won't say 'I'm on the dog and bone.'

Or you'd just refer to someone as Hampton, not Hampton wick.

That just makes it feel too forced and puts me off.
